Some explanation text retains per-question OCR uncertainty where the answer/explanation pages were visually degraded.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother for a routine well-child examination. Her mother expresses concern that her daughter refuses to comply with her usual bedtime and frequently stays awake reading well past the time that her parents have set for her to go to sleep. Occasionally, she wakes up tired and does not want to go to school. Her mother reports that her daughter has become more argumentative lately and has begun to question the family rules. She was treated with methylphenidate for 2 years beginning at the age of 7 years for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Her pulse is 64/min, and blood pressure is 102/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild acneiform lesions over the face and neck. There is no evidence of jaundice. On mental status ‘examination, she is mildly anxious. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Measurement of serum thyrotropin-releasing hormone concentration

questions.educationalObjective: In normal development, adolescents form their own identity and may separate from their parents by expressing different opinions and breaking rules. Her rebellion is volitional, not impulsive, and reflects the development of identity.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is demonstrating behaviors reflective of normal development. It would be appropriate for the physician to provide education and reassurance that the patient's current behavior is normal. According to the Erikson stages of psychosocial development, the task of adolescents is to formulate their identity as an independent person. This task involves recognizing that they are separate from their parents by expressing their own opinions and preferences. A degree of teenage rebellion may be expressed by questioning family rules.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Family therapy (Choice A) is indicated for oppositional defiant disorder or conduct disorder. This patient is exhibiting normal adolescent behavior.
Individual psychotherapy (Choice B) is indicated for many psychiatric disorders, including depression and anxiety; however, individual psychotherapy is not indicated.

Measurement of serum thyrotropin-releasing hormone concentration (Choice C) helps distinguish causes of physical examination abnormalities (eg, hair or skin changes) consistent with thyroid dysfunction.
Polysomnography (Choice E) helps diagnose causes of unrestful sleep such as sleep apnea. This patient has normal adolescent behavior.

Reinstitute methylphenidate therapy (Choice F) would be indicated for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder with symptoms such as inattention, hyperactive, or impulsive symptoms. Her rebellion is volitional, not impulsive, and reflects the development of identity.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of daily thoughts of attacking her 2-year-old son with a knife. She is very distressed by these thoughts and cannot get the images out of her mind, but she is convinced that she would never actually harm her child. She has no history of serious medical or psychiatric illness and takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is cooperative. She has an anxious mood; she says she is not depressed. She is oriented to person, place, and time. Her thought process is logical and coherent. There is no evidence of auditory or visual hallucinations. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive disorder manifests as recurrent, intrusive, anxiety-inducing urges or thoughts that are relieved by repetitive rituals (compulsions). Many patients demonstrate both obsessions and compulsions, but some experience obsessions only. Obsessions about harm to others are common.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive, anxiety-inducing urges or thoughts that are relieved by repetitive rituals (compulsions). These symptoms cause significant distress and/or daily dysfunction. Common obsessions include harm to self or others (as in this patient), and taboo thoughts or ideas. Compulsions are rigid repetitive activities (eg, counting, repeating words) that relieve anxiety related to obsessions. Some patients experience either obsessions or compulsions only. OCD typically presents relatively early in life. Treatment is cognitive behavioral therapy and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors such as paroxetine. Clomipramine and venlafaxine are alternatives.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Buspirone (Choice A) is a serotonin modulator useful for generalized anxiety disorder. It is not helpful for OCD.
Cyproheptadine (Choice B) is a serotonin antagonist used to treat serotonin syndrome. A serotonin antagonist is not indicated.
Lorazepam (Choice C) is unlikely to be helpful. Benzodiazepines have no proven efficacy for OCD.
Risperidone (Choice E) has a small amount of evidence for refractory OCD. However, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are first-line therapy.

questions.retrievalTag: Haloperidol acute dystonic reaction dopamine blockade
questions.stem: One day after undergoing an elective cholecystectomy, a 72-year-old woman becomes confused and belligerent. She has a 2-year history of mild dementia, Alzheimer type. She takes no medications. Intramuscular haloperidol is administered, and 1 hour later, she begins to repetitively hyperextend her neck. She also has repetitive conjugate upward movements of the eyes that she says she cannot control. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: An acute dystonic reaction most commonly occurs in response to potent dopamine antagonists and may present with torticollis, retrocollis, opisthotonos, and/or oculogyric crisis. Treatment involves anticholinergic medications that work to restore the dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Acute dystonic reactions are involuntary, painful muscle spasms that occur most frequently in response to antipsychotics (eg, haloperidol). It is postulated that an imbalance of dopamine (a movement facilitator) and acetylcholine (a movement inhibitor) contributes to acute dystonic reaction. Common presentations include torticollis (head tilted to one side), opisthotonos (arching of the back), and oculogyric crisis (involuntary deviation of the eyes). Patients rarely have altered consciousness. The pathophysiology is an imbalance in the dopaminergic-cholinergic pathways of the basal ganglia. Risk factors include male sex, young age, and a history of previous acute dystonic reaction. Acute dystonic reactions are treated with parenteral anticholinergic medications that work to restore the proper balance in the dopaminergic and cholinergic pathways.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acetylcholine blockade (Choice A), such as with benztropine or diphenhydramine therapy, treats acute dystonia rather than causing acute dystonic reaction.

Acetylcholine excess (Choice B) is not associated with acute dystonic reaction. It is thought that an imbalance between dopamine and acetylcholine action, though dopamine antagonism much more commonly leads to acute dystonic reaction.
Dopamine excess (Choice D) in the nigrostriatal pathway is associated with excess movement.
Serotonin blockade (Choice E) is associated with antiemetic effects. Serotonin excess (Choice F) is associated with serotonin syndrome and movement regulation.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of sadness and difficulty falling asleep since he and his wife separated 3 weeks ago. He has had no changes in energy or appetite. He enjoys spending time with friends, which he says distracts him from the separation. He is hopeful that he and his wife will work through their conflicts with therapy and time apart. He has no history of serious illness. His sister has major depressive disorder well controlled with sertraline. Physical ‘examination of the patient shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, his mood is sad, and he is tearful. His thought process is logical. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation or hallucinations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Patients may experience associated depression or anxiety symptoms but do not meet the diagnostic criteria for another condition.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor that is out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily school, work, or social functioning. Anxiety, depression, or behavioral symptoms without meeting the criteria of anxiety or depressive disorder may occur, but no anhedonia or changes in energy or appetite. As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, management includes supportive psychotherapy but may also include symptomatic treatment of anxiety or depressive symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance, and/or negative mood after a life-threatening motor vehicle collision. The traumatic event is defined as serious physical or actual death. This patient was not in this context.

Bereavement (Choice C), or uncomplicated grief reaction, refers to a person's emotional response after the death of a loved one, with difficulty concentrating, decreased interest in usual activities, preoccupation with thoughts and memories of the deceased, and changes such as sleep, appetite, and energy disturbances. This patient did not experience the death of a loved one.

Conversion disorder (Choice D), also referred to as functional neurological symptom disorder, can result in neurological symptoms not explained by a physical illness or injury. This patient does not have neurologic symptoms or show neurological deficits.

Major depressive disorder (Choice E) includes 2 or more weeks of five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms. This patient does not have enough symptoms to meet major depressive disorder criteria.

extractionWarnings: Some wording in Incorrect Answers Choice A is partially truncated by OCR.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman is brought to the office by her mother for a pregnancy test because the patient claims that she is carrying the child of a famous rock star. She says she had not told her parents about the relationship because of her boyfriend's need for privacy. She also says that he cannot call her at home but has regularly talked to her through a television newscaster during the past week. Her excitement at becoming pregnant prompted her to tell her parents. During the past 2 months, they have noticed behavior changes in their daughter, including collecting pictures of the star, spending excessive amounts of money on makeup and clothing, sleeping little, and becoming increasingly impatient and demanding with her family and friends. There is a family history of type 1 diabetes mellitus and major depressive disorder. The patient's temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 73/min, and blood pressure is 105/68 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. A pregnancy test is negative. Toxicology screening is negative. When told that she is not pregnant, the patient is adamant that the test results are wrong. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that features acute episodes of mania and depression. Mania manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased energy, decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking. Some patients demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, erotomanic delusions).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is experiencing an episode of acute mania and can therefore be diagnosed with bipolar disorder. Bipolar disorder includes episodes of mania and may include episodes of depression. Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric mood, decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility (as evidenced by spending, sexual activity), and patients may demonstrate associated psychotic symptoms (eg, erotomanic delusions as in this patient). Management of acute mania typically requires mood stabilizing medication and antipsychotics.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance, and/or negative mood after a traumatic event. This patient has not experienced a traumatic event recently, and her symptoms are more consistent with mania.

Dissociative identity disorder (Choice C), formerly known as multiple personality disorder, refers to patients who experience day to day, and is frequently related to childhood trauma. This patient's behavior change is more prolonged and consistent with bipolar disorder.

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (Choice D) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions and compulsions or attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). This patient demonstrates an erotomanic delusion without evidence otherwise. Patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder demonstrate more cognitive thoughts rather than fixed thoughts being false. As well, decreased need for sleep and irresponsible behaviors are more consistent with mania.

Schizophrenia (Choice E) presents with at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). This patient does not demonstrate negative symptoms, and her mood symptoms (decreased need for sleep) consistent with bipolar disorder.

extractionWarnings: First character of age is partially unclear but appears to be 18-year-old.
Some wording in Incorrect Answers Choice C is partially truncated by OCR.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with alcohol use disorder is brought to the emergency department because of double vision and an inability to walk for 1 day. Examination shows sluggish pupillary response and horizontal nystagmus. There are palsies of the lateral rectus muscles bilaterally. He is disoriented to place and time. He falls when he attempts to walk. The most likely underlying cause of these findings is a deficiency of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol use disorder and associated malnutrition may result in vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency with Wernicke encephalopathy is characterized by altered mental status, ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia. Treatment with intravenous thiamine to prevent progression to Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic memory disorder with the consequent tendency to confabulate.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients with alcohol use disorder typically have a significant vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency due to the poor nutrition and poor utilization of thiamine. Over time, thiamine deficiency can result in damage and atrophy of mammillary bodies and is known as Wernicke encephalopathy. The typical clinical presentation of Wernicke encephalopathy is altered mental status, ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia. Patients may present with physical examination findings and laboratory findings. The symptoms of Wernicke encephalopathy are reversible but increase to a condition known as Korsakoff psychosis in patients with alcohol use disorder if not immediately treated with thiamine. This is an irreversible condition that features anterograde and retrograde amnesia along with the consequent tendency to confabulate to compensate for the inability to remember.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Deficiency of folic acid (Choice A) or vitamin B6 is relatively common in patients with alcohol use disorder and nutritional dysfunction. Folic acid deficiency more commonly manifests as macrocytic anemia and hyperhomocysteinemia.
Niacin deficiency (Choice B) results in pellagra, which classically presents with the triad of dementia, diarrhea, and dermatitis.
Vitamin B6 deficiency (Choice D) typically presents with microcytic anemia, dermatitis, glossitis, cheilosis, depression, neuropathy, ophthalmoplegia, and nystagmus would be atypical.

Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency (Choice E) may occur in patients with alcohol use disorder but typically manifests with sensory deficits, ataxia, weakness, and numbness (due to demyelination of the spinocerebellar tracts, lateral corticospinal tract, and dorsal column), anemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, and increased methylmalonic acid. Ophthalmoplegia would be atypical.

extractionWarnings: Educational objective grammar preserved as visible OCR, but OCR may have omitted punctuation or connecting words.

questions.retrievalTag: Psychosis with firearm outpatient safety planning
questions.stem: 42-year-old man is brought to the office by his wife because of a 1-month history of progressive irritability and paranoia. His e-mail to her yesterday contained violent song lyrics and uncharacteristically rambling, paranoid statements about his parents. Four months ago, he bought a handgun. He has never threatened to harm anyone. His wife says she feels safe but worries that he will unintentionally “do something stupid.” She says he has always acted grandiose and promotes himself as a rock band manager, but he is “really just a groupie.” He is currently angry at a local rock band that he says owes him money for his services. His parents financially support him, his wife, and two children. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On examination, he is cooperative, occasionally scans the room, and blinks every 3 seconds. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has an annoyed mood and blunted affect. His thought process is circumferential; his answers are long and complex. When asked about the e-mail to his wife, he laughs and says she misunderstood it. He reports no hallucinations or suicidal or homicidal thoughts. He is able to recall three out of three items after 2 minutes and is fully oriented. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to reduce this patient's potential danger to himself or to others?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Arrange to remove the weapon from the patient's home

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Have the patient sign a contract for safety

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Notify the patient's parents and the rock band about his potential for danger

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Report the patient's behavior to child protective services

questions.educationalObjective: When a patient has several acute risk factors for suicide and/or homicide, hospitalization may be considered. If no active ideation, outpatient safety planning can be pursued. Restricted access to lethal means, especially firearms, is important.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient has several acute risk factors for suicide and homicide, indicating that his access to lethal means is a concern. His paranoid ideation, increased impulsivity, and possession of a firearm. It is also concerning that the patient is worried by his song choices. His acute risk factors for suicide include increased impulsivity and possession of a firearm. If at high risk for suicide and homicide, psychiatric hospitalization should be considered. However, given his lack of current suicidal or homicidal ideation, it is reasonable to create an outpatient safety plan. An important element is reducing access to means such as firearms. The physician should attempt to obtain buy-in from the patient to dispose of the weapon. To reduce acute safety risk, antipsychotic medication should be started if the patient is agreeable, and the cause of psychosis should be assessed.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Administering aripiprazole (Choice A) may begin to address the patient's psychosis within days to weeks but would not reduce immediate risk.
Have the patient sign a contract for safety (Choice C) refers to agreements that a patient signs promising not to engage in violence. These are ineffective in preventing violence.
Notify the patient's parents and the rock band about his potential for danger (Choice D) is not needed as he has not made specific threats toward his parents or the rock band.

Report the patient's behavior to child protective services (Choice E) does not address the immediate risk to the patient. This is not necessary at this time since the patient has not expressed paranoia or threats toward his children.

extractionWarnings: Several phrases in the stem are OCR-corrupted or semantically unclear; preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man, who is a college student, comes to the office because of a 1-year history of progressive feelings of anxiety when he uses stairs or drives an automobile. He reports that when he climbs a flight of stairs at school, he becomes afraid that he will kick something onto the stairs that will cause someone else to fall. When he drives over a bump in his car, he is afraid that he ran someone over. In both situations, his anxiety and fear build until he returns and checks to ensure that no one is hurt. He often has to repeat this behavior several times. He says that he has been late for class on numerous occasions because he avoids staircases. He reports that he has few friends and prefers the company of his parents. His parents are aware of his symptoms and have encouraged him to seek help. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears mildly anxious and expresses concern about accidentally hurting someone. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions (repetitive anxiety-inducing thoughts) that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). OCD is treated with psychotherapy and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) manifests as recurrent, intrusive urges or thoughts (obsessions) that are relieved by repetitive behaviors (compulsions). Common obsessions are related to cleanliness, symmetry, harm to self or others, or taboo thoughts. Repetitive behaviors (eg, checking, washing) or mental activities (eg, counting, repeating words) that relieve anxiety qualify as compulsions. OCD symptoms can cause significant anxiety to the patient and markedly impair functioning (eg, lead to lateness, as in this patient). Patients may also experience anxiety or major mood disorders. OCD typically presents relatively early in life, with treatment including cognitive behavioral therapy as well as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor medications.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice A) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a variety of issues and associated functional impairment. This patient's repetitive, intrusive thoughts about harm to others are more specific.

Panic disorder (Choice C) is an anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and absence of panic attack triggers. This patient's anxiety is not unexpected; it is predictably triggered by a specific situation.

Social anxiety disorder (social phobia) (Choice D) refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or humiliation causing significant distress and/or impaired functioning. Though this patient prefers his parents' company, he is not afraid of scrutiny. Instead, this patient's anxiety centers around harming others.

Specific phobia (Choice E) is an anxiety disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that triggers avoidance. Checking is consistent with a compulsion. As well, this patient's anxiety is triggered by an obsession, not a specific situation.

extractionWarnings: One stem sentence appears OCR-corrupted: 'He avoids stairs because he avoids staircases.'

questions.retrievalTag: Adolescent sense of invulnerability pregnancy risk
questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl comes to the clinic for a pregnancy test. She is not sure about the date of her last menstrual period. She has been sexually active for 6 months, and she and her sexual partner occasionally use condoms. She does not use contraception because, "It hasn't happened yet, and | don't really think it could happen to me." She thinks that she cannot get pregnant without an orgasm. She will not use contraceptive jellies or spermicidal foams because "they are gross.” This patient's behavior and statements illustrate which of the following characteristics of adolescent development?
questions.educationalObjective: A sense of invulnerability, arising from the beginnings of the formal operational stage, can describe the ability of children to use thinking and learning to navigate the world.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient demonstrates a sense of invulnerability, consistent with the principle of adolescent egocentrism. During development, adolescents enter the formal operational stage and begin thinking abstractly (eg, using logic). During this stage, they experience their thoughts as powerful and begin to direct their thoughts inward toward themselves. By over-differentiating their own thoughts from others' thoughts, they presume that others are focused on their thoughts as much as they are and special. This sense of uniqueness is hypothesized to contribute to a sense of invulnerability in adolescents. Research studies on the validity of this theory of adolescent invulnerability have been mixed. Piaget's cognitive development theory can be used to describe how children use thinking and learning to navigate the world. The stages progress over time across the sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete, and formal operational stages.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Abstract thinking (Choice A) is beginning to develop in adolescents. However, the lack of fully developed abstract thinking and differences between their thoughts and others' thoughts. As described above, this lack of experience may lead to errors.
Concrete thinking (Choice B) refers to reasoning based on perceptual information about the physical world and inability to consider the hypothetical situation of pregnancy.
Delusional thinking (Choice C) involves fixed false beliefs. This patient does not convey complete certainty.
Mature sexuality (Choice D) is not a widely adopted concept. It is thought that there is not one universal progression of steps to sexual maturity in her cognitive approach to sex.

extractionWarnings: The quotation 'it could happen to me' may be OCR-corrupted because it conflicts with the highlighted answer; preserved as visible text.
Some Incorrect Answers wording is partially OCR-corrupted.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her friends 20 minutes after losing consciousness at a party. Just before losing consciousness, she began to slur her words. Her friends say that there were alcohol, benzodiazepines, hallucinogens, narcotics, and stimulants at the party. They think that she was using one of these substances, but they do not know which one. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 9/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. She responds to sternal rub with moaning and is unable to answer questions. The pupils are 1 to 2 mm and minimally reactive to light. Examination shows no nystagmus or involuntary movements. Use of which of the following substances is the most likely cause of these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Opioid intoxication causes symptoms of central nervous system (CNS) depression and respiratory depression. Miosis differentiates opioid intoxication from other CNS depressant toxidromes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely intoxicated with heroin, an opioid drug. Opioids, which are central nervous system depressants, act throughout the CNS and peripheral nervous system and interact with several neurotransmitters. They can cause euphoria due to interaction with dopamine, altered mental status, sedation, bradycardia and hypotension. Miosis is a distinctive finding that is less common in intoxication with other CNS depressants and is caused by stimulation of the pupillary control. Opioids also act on receptors within the enteric nervous system, decreasing gastrointestinal motility.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Alcohol (Choice A) and clonazepam (Choice B) are CNS depressants that can cause slurred speech, confusion, sedation, stupor and respiratory depression can also occur. This patient's miosis makes the presentation more consistent with opioid intoxication.

Cocaine (Choice C) is an addictive recreational drug that typically causes euphoria, restlessness, and increased sympathetic activity. This patient has miosis, not mydriasis, making cocaine less likely.

LSD (Choice E) is a potent hallucinogen known to cause auditory and visual hallucinations, depersonalization, and derealization. PCP (Choice F) can cause similar symptoms to LSD and is also associated with aggression, rotatory nystagmus, and analgesia. This patient's miosis makes hallucinogen intoxication less likely.

questions.stem: Four days after open reduction and internal fixation of a tibial fracture, a 57-year-old woman becomes agitated. She believes that the nurses are experimenting on her and are not letting her sleep. She has hypertension and systemic lupus erythematosus. She has no history of psychiatric illness or substance abuse. Her medications include chloroquine, hydrochlorothiazide, meperidine, propranolol, and thyroxine. She appears tremulous. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person and place but not to time. She says that the nurses are trying to poison her. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition and may also present with perceptual disturbances such as paranoid delusions. Meperidine is one of the medications associated with delirium.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Delirium is an acute confusional state typically resulting from acute medical illness, sleep disturbances, or medications associated with delirium include anticholinergics, opioids, and benzodiazepines. Of opioid medications, meperidine is most problematic. Delirium presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition often with perceptual disturbances (eg, delusions as in this patient, hallucinations). Management includes withdrawing the offending medication, optimizing exacerbating medical conditions, frequent reorientation, maintenance of a sleep-wake cycle, and soothing environment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Chloroquine (Choice A) is an antimalarial agent used to treat systemic lupus erythematosus. Hydrochlorothiazide (Choice B) is used to treat hypertension.

Propranolol (Choice D) is a beta blocker used to treat tremor, performance anxiety, and hypertension. These medications are associated with delirium (ie, none are found on the Beers Criteria medication list).
Thyroxine (Choice E) is used for thyroid replacement therapy and is not the most likely cause of this patient's acute confusional state.

questions.retrievalTag: Somatic symptom disorder chronic pain preoccupation
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the office as a new patient because of a 3-year history of constant abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting, and diarrhea that have become acutely more severe during the past 2 weeks. She also reports constant headache; painful, irregular, menstrual periods with heavy bleeding; occasional chest pain; and pain during intercourse, She reports, "| am in pain 24 hours a day.” Sometimes her headaches are so intense during times of stress that she becomes "temporarily blind.” She states that she has seen nearly a dozen physicians since her symptoms started because "they don't seem to know what's wrong with me. | am a really complicated case. | need someone of your caliber and education.” Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows shifting areas of tenderness throughout the abdomen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: In somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that they disrupt daily life. These symptoms may or may not originate from an underlying disease though are typically unrelated to stress.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Somatic symptom disorder is the most likely cause for this patient's pain symptoms in the setting of ongoing symptoms and a negative psychiatric assessment. According to the DSM-5, patients with somatic symptom disorder have somatic symptoms that disrupt the patient's daily life. Somatic symptoms are thought to represent the unconscious conversion of emotional distress. As such, a trauma history is common. These symptoms may or may not originate from underlying disease. Patients are often preoccupied by mood or anxiety disorder diagnoses. Patients with somatic symptom disorder persistently devote excessive time and health concerns (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor). Somatic symptom disorder typically demonstrates improvement if treatment centers around regular primary care follow-up that targets coping skills, provides reassurance, and avoids unnecessary testing.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Functional neurological symptom disorder (Choice A) involves neurologic symptoms such as sensory or motor symptoms with physical examination or imaging. Common examples include blindness, aphasia, and unexplained weakness. These are thought to represent the subconscious conversion of intolerable emotional distress into neurologic symptoms.

Histrionic personality disorder (Choice B), a cluster B personality disorder, is characterized by theatrical, seductive behavior and attention from others to fulfill emotional needs. Patients may behave seductively. This patient's preoccupation is with her pain, not seduction.

Illness anxiety disorder (Choice C) is characterized by excessive anxiety and preoccupation about the possibility of having or acquiring a serious illness. Patients may perform frequent health-related behaviors (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor) or exhibit maladaptive avoidance. This patient is preoccupied with her existing pain symptoms.

Major depressive disorder (Choice D) includes two or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms. This patient does not report depressive symptoms.

Psychological factors affecting medical condition (Choice E) refers to psychological or behavioral factors that adversely affect a medical condition. Though psychological factors contribute to the generation of her somatic symptoms, no medical condition is described.

extractionWarnings: Some stem wording around symptom list is OCR-corrupted.

questions.retrievalTag: Normal sleep changes with aging reassurance
questions.stem: A 74-year-old widower comes to the physician because of gradually increasing insomnia over the past 2 years. He goes to bed at 11:30 pm and awakens at 5:00 am. He sleeps soundly except for awakening to go to the bathroom once during the night. He is concerned because he used to sleep 8 hours daily. He is active and prepares his own meals. Medications include ranitidine at bedtime for gastroesophageal reflux disease and verapamil for hypertension. His pulse today is 88/min, and blood pressure is 160/88 mm Hg. He appears mildly anxious, Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Reassure the patient that his sleep pattern is normal for his age

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Recommend staying up later to avoid getting up so early

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend an over-the-counter drug containing diphenhydramine at bedtime

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Prescribe temazepam at bedtime, as needed

questions.educationalObjective: Normal sleep changes with age include a slightly decreased total sleep duration, decreased deep and REM sleep, and increased nighttime awakenings. Reassurance is sufficient for patients with normal age-related sleep changes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Adults typically experience several sleep-related changes as they age. A slightly decreased total sleep duration, decreased nighttime awakenings (three per night is normal), and a decreased proportion of time spent in deep sleep and REM sleep. Increase in nighttime awakenings can be attributed to less time spent in deep sleep and nocturia (as in this patient). Management should include reassurance, and counseling on sleep hygiene can help patients optimize sleep.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Recommending staying up later to avoid getting up so early (Choice B) would be unnecessary in this patient. Sleep hygiene includes staying up until sleepiness occurs and waking up at the same time each morning.

Recommending warm milk before bedtime (Choice C) may be appropriately suggested later, as positive associations can help with sleepiness. However, this patient who is anxious about his sleep patterns should first be reassured.

Recommending diphenhydramine (Choice D) or prescribing temazepam (Choice E) would be inappropriate. Antihistamine and benzodiazepine medications should be used with caution in elderly patients because they increase risk for confusion, falls.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because he is worried about speaking at a convention in a few weeks. He reports that he ruminates constantly about disappointing his boss. He says that when he practices his speech, he blushes, has sweaty hands and a racing pulse, and feels embarrassed. He does not go to parties or date because he is uncomfortable talking with people he does not know. He says that he feels conspicuous eating at restaurants because he imagines that other patrons are amused by him, and he has difficulty signing checks in front of a bank teller. His pulse is 86/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Examination shows hyperhidrosis. The hands are chapped, and the fingernails are extensively bitten. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. An ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Social anxiety disorder (social phobia) refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny or negative evaluation that leads to avoidance behavior. Treatment typically involves cognitive behavioral therapy and antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Social anxiety disorder (social phobia) refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection that causes distress and/or impaired functioning. Patients with social anxiety disorder commonly exhibit anticipatory anxiety, fear of negative evaluation in situations such as public speaking, test taking, and sex. Patients' fear may cause avoidance or limiting. Patients with social anxiety disorder may experience decreased anxiety when interacting with familiar people and difficulty socializing in groups or with strangers. Body-focused behaviors (eg, nail-biting) are common in patients with social anxiety disorder. Treatment typically involves cognitive behavioral therapy and antidepressant medication (eg, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Delusional disorder (Choice A) is a psychotic disorder that features the presence of one or more delusions. This patient demonstrates anxiety about the possibility of social rejection rather than a fixed, false belief.

Generalized anxiety disorder (Choice B) is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a variety of issues and associated functional impairment. This patient's anxiety is specific to social situations.

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (Choice C) is an anxiety disorder typically characterized by obsessions that the patient attempts to neutralize with compulsions (repetitive behaviors). This patient does not demonstrate compulsive behaviors and his anxiety is social.
Agoraphobia (Choice D) refers to the fear of leaving one's home. Though the patient reports fears of being embarrassed, he does not primarily fear leaving his home.

Paranoid personality disorder (Choice E) is a cluster A personality disorder, the odd or eccentric cluster, characterized by suspiciousness and others' intentions. Though the patient fears others will negatively evaluate him, he is not suspicious of malicious intent.

extractionWarnings: Some stem wording is OCR-corrupted, including phrase 'He has no delusions because he imagines...'; preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman comes to the emergency department (ED) because of a broken arm that she says happened from a fall she sustained. The patient's medical record shows that this is her third visit to the ED during the past 6 months; the previous two visits were for a broken ankle and a concussion. When asked about her recent injuries, she states, "I am sorry, but | am so clumsy.” She states that she used to work as teacher but has stayed home since the birth of her third child 8 months ago. She says that the loss of her income has put a significant financial strain on the family and has required her husband to work two jobs. She has no history of psychiatric illness or significant medical history. She is alert and slightly disheveled. Her mood is mildly anxious and she often looks at her husband before answering any questions. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a swollen right forearm and several bruises on her upper extremities and stomach; when asked about these bruises, the patient says that she often bumps into things. Which of the following is the most appropriate first course of action for the physician to take?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Admit the patient to an inpatient psychiatric facility

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Develop an emergency safety plan with the patient

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Recommend anger management therapy to the patient's husband

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Recommend the patient file an order of protection from her husband

questions.educationalObjective: Domestic abuse should be suspected in patients who present with frequent injuries and/or bruises on the lower abdomen, back, or forearms. Pregnant women, children, and the elderly are vulnerable victims. Patients who are unwilling or unable to report their abuser should be encouraged to develop an emergency safety plan.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Domestic abuse or intimate partner violence (IPV) is the most likely explanation for this patient's distribution of injuries. IPV should be considered in patients with a suspicious pattern of bruising and/or explanations for bruising inconsistent with physical examination findings. Areas of bruising that are particularly worrying include the lower abdomen, back, or forearms bruised while employing defensive movements to prevent injury to the face or torso during an attack. Emotional instability and substance use. IPV often increases or begins during pregnancy, especially unwanted pregnancy. Patients suspicious for IPV should be asked in a tactful and nonthreatening manner if they feel safe at home and if any intimidation that may occur when the partner is present. If patients express that they are being abused but are unwilling or unable to act. Often, patients are not yet willing or able to leave a violent relationship and understand risks. The doctor should support all patients should receive assistance with developing a safety plan, which includes identifying safe places to stay, secret words to children or neighbors who can call for help, and preparing an emergency kit stored outside the home that contains basic patient needs to flee. Written materials such as pamphlets should generally be avoided as these can be found by abusers. Mandatory reporting for the abuse of children, elderly, and disabled persons, but requirements for reporting adult IPV vary by state.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Admitting the patient to an inpatient psychiatric facility (Choice A) is incorrect. While this patient is in danger, she does not have a psychiatric condition. Admission to an inpatient facility would only temporarily remove her from danger and worsen if she is able.

Contacting the police (Choice B) may be required in some states where adult abuse is included in mandatory reporting IPV. Involving law enforcement or recommending the patient file an order of protection from her husband before she is ready may only escalate the situation and increase the risk for harm.

Recommending anger management therapy to the patient's husband (Choice D) is incorrect. Intervention with the abuser or anger management therapy has not conclusively been shown to decrease the risk for violence.
Recommending the patient file an order of protection from her husband (Choice E) may escalate the situation and increase the risk for harm if done before the patient is ready.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation text is OCR-corrupted and preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-month history of increased difficulty maintaining an erection during sexual intercourse. One year ago, he was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus. He also has a 40-year history of obsessive-compulsive disorder. Medications are sertraline, clomipramine, risperidone, and metformin. He has been married for the past 28 years. The patient drinks five beers weekly. He is 190 cm (6 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 97 kg (215 Ib); BMI is 27 kg/m?, Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 130/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild gynecomastia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: By antagonizing dopamine receptors in the tuberoinfundibular pathway, risperidone can cause hyperprolactinemia and sexual dysfunction in males. Male patients on risperidone therapy can present with gynecomastia, galactorrhea, and reduced testosterone.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Risperidone is an atypical antipsychotic medication, used occasionally in refractory obsessive-compulsive disorder. It antagonizes dopamine in the mesolimbic, nigrostriatal, and tuberoinfundibular pathways, corresponding to psychotic, extrapyramidal symptoms, and sexual dysfunction, respectively. In the tuberoinfundibular pathway, dopamine inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. Of the antipsychotic medications, risperidone is associated with increased hyperprolactinemia. Prolactin suppresses gonadotropin-releasing hormone, which decreases release of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone. Hyperprolactinemia can cause sexual dysfunction. In males, decreased testicular release of testosterone leads to erectile dysfunction and decreased libido. In females, this is associated with amenorrhea and infertility. If antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinemia is suspected in patients with hyperprolactinemia, as in this patient, options include discontinuing risperidone, adding a dopamine agonist, or replacing with aripiprazole.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Alcohol consumption (Choice A), if chronic and heavy, can lead to decreased libido and impaired vasodilation and erectile dysfunction. However, this patient's alcohol consumption is moderate (ie, within the Centers for Disease Control limit of 14 drinks weekly for males).
Diabetic neuropathy (Choice B) typically develops after several years of poorly controlled diabetes mellitus.

Metformin therapy (Choice C) can occasionally lead to decreased testosterone and, consequently, decreased libido. However, gynecomastia, which is typically mediated by hyperprolactinemia, would be uncommon.

Sertraline therapy (Choice E) can commonly cause sexual dysfunction within all three phases of sexual function and orgasmic effects. However, gynecomastia, which is typically mediated by hyperprolactinemia, would be uncommon.

extractionWarnings: Medication list syntax in stem is OCR-corrupted; preserved from visible text.
Blood pressure value is not visible in parsed text and was not inferred.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-month history of falling asleep during school. He has mucopolysaccharidosis III and attends special education classes. His teacher notes that she has found him asleep at his desk on two occasions and is concerned that he may have had unwitnessed seizures. He has hyperactive and aggressive behavior treated with haloperidol. He also receives diphenhydramine at bedtime to help him fall asleep. He snores loudly and tosses and turns throughout the night. He awakens with a headache each morning that resolves quickly after arising. Examination shows multiple joint contractures and coarse facial features and hair. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
questions.educationalObjective: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness and upper airway obstruction. Mucopolysaccharidoses, are a rare cause of OSA. Diagnosis involves polysomnography, with enzyme replacement therapy or surgical correction.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Polysomnography is used to diagnose obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), which is likely in this patient. He has sleep disturbances with untreated OSA. This patient is likely experiencing sleep-related hypoventilation due to OSA causing excessive daytime fatigue, falling asleep at inappropriate times, loud snoring, and a disrupted sleep-wake cycle. Risk factors include anatomical abnormalities of the oropharynx and upper airway, neuromuscular disorders, and use of sedating medications. Management commonly involves adenotonsillar surgery for cases with obstructing lesions. Children with mucopolysaccharidosis III can also respond well to enzyme replacement therapy. Mucopolysaccharidoses result from enzyme deficiency that prevents the degradation of glycosaminoglycans in the lysosome, leading to cellular dysfunction. Patients demonstrate progressive central nervous system degeneration (eg, developmental delay, behavioral problems), coarse facial features, joint contractures, and hepatosplenomegaly. Sleep disorders such as sleep apnea are common.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Complete blood count (Choice A) is unlikely to be helpful in diagnosing this patient's sleep episodes. Mucopolysaccharidoses, and anemia typically presents with fatigue, not sleepiness.

EEG (Choice B) may assist in diagnosing seizures. However, this patient does not demonstrate evidence of seizures, and episodes of sleeping at his desk are likely secondary to daytime sleepiness from OSA.

CT scan of the head (Choice D) and lumbar puncture (Choice E) are helpful in diagnosing hydrocephalus or infection in mucopolysaccharidoses. Patients may present with a morning headache and other sleep disturbances, and progressive neurologic dysfunction. Other than morning headaches, this sleepiness is best explained by OSA.

extractionWarnings: The phrase 'and witnessed seizures' is possibly OCR-corrupted; preserved as visible text.

questions.retrievalTag: Major depressive disorder pseudodementia older adult
questions.stem: An 82-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine examination. For the past 2 months, he has felt fatigued and has slept only 4 to 5 hours nightly. He has stopped taking part in church and social activities. He reports that his appetite is "pretty good, but food just doesn't taste the same." He has had a 2.3-kg (5-Ib) weight loss since his last visit 6 months ago. He has hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and peptic ulcer disease. Current medications include ranitidine, terazosin, and hydrochlorothiazide. He is 185 cm (6 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (155 Ib); BMI is 20 kg/m2, His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 82/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 160/96 mm Hg. Examination shows 1+ pedal edema. There is a mild tremor of the outstretched hands. The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, his eyes are downcast. He speaks slowly and softly with a prolonged response latency and has difficulty concentrating on the questions. His mood is nonreactive. He is oriented to person and place. He knows the month, year, and day of the week but not the day of the month. He recalls two of three words after 5 minutes and can draw an intact clock face correctly. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Mood disorder secondary to a general medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: Symptoms of major depressive disorder include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, loss of interest, psychomotor slowing, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms. Mental status examination findings commonly include a depressed affect with a decreased affective range, poor eye contact, psychomotor slowing, and distractibility.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient meets criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD). MDD is characterized by 2 or more weeks of depressed mood or anhedonia (eg, decreased hedonic value of food), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal ideation, and neurovegetative symptoms (decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). These symptoms have been present for 2 months. Examination findings commonly include a depressed affect with a decreased affective range, poor eye contact, psychomotor slowing, and distractibility. Mild abnormalities on cognitive testing may be present, particularly in older patients. Patients with depression have high suicide completion rates, particularly in older men. The most effective treatment includes psychotherapy and antidepressant medication.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood (Choice A) refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and depressive symptoms that do not meet the diagnostic criteria for MDD. This patient meets the diagnostic criteria for MDD.
Dysthymic disorder (Choice B), also known as persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia), refers to depressive symptoms that last for more than 2 years and do not meet MDD criteria.
Mood disorder secondary to a general medical condition (Choice D) is unlikely in this patient. Physiologic changes can occur in older adults and typically do not suggest a serious medical illness.
Normal aging (Choice E) includes a slow decline of executive functioning, processing speed, episodic memory, and divided attention but is not a part of normal aging.

extractionWarnings: Some wording in the stem is OCR-corrupted, including '6 months ago' and orientation sentence; preserved as visible text.

questions.stem: 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police because of strange behavior after he was arrested for disturbing the residents in his neighborhood. For the past 6 months, he has been aggressively advertising for his New Age healing business by distributing leaflets. He runs the business from his house and claims that he has special abilities to cure all kinds of physical and mental ailments. He is extremely upset about being arrested and believes that the physician and police are conspiring against him and his business. He says, "You don't understand doc! | really have special powers, and | can heal all the people in the waiting room.” Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he says that he has not had any psychiatric problems, depression, elevated mood, or auditory hallucinations. He sleeps 6 to 8 hours nightly. Laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delusional disorder is a psychotic disorder that features the presence of one or more delusions for 1 month without other prominent psychotic symptoms. Treatment commonly includes antipsychotic medication and psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Delusional disorder is a psychotic disorder that features the presence of one or more delusions for 1 month. Delusions, disorganized thinking, disorganized behaviors other than behaviors related to the delusion, and prominent hallucinations and somatic delusions are common types of delusions. Many patients will only present for care due to concern about psychotic nature of their concern. Treatment typically includes antipsychotic medication and psychotherapy. Focus on the delusion and gradually increasing insight into the delusion. The prognosis is guarded.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Bipolar disorder (Choice A) is characterized by discrete episodes of depression and mania, which can cause hallucinations and delusions. This patient does not report mood symptoms (eg, depressed or euphoric mood).

Brief psychotic disorder (Choice B) is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations) lasting less than 1 month. This patient's delusion has persisted for 6 months.

Schizoaffective disorder (Choice D) and schizophrenia (Choice E) are chronic psychotic disorders that present with disorganized behavior, and negative symptoms (eg, alogia, apathy, flat affect). Schizoaffective disorder also includes mood episodes. This patient demonstrates an isolated delusion with no disorganization of speech/thinking or negative symptoms.

extractionWarnings: Quoted sentence in stem is OCR-corrupted and preserved as best-effort visible text.
Some explanation wording is OCR-corrupted.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the physician as a new patient for a routine health maintenance examination. His mother reports that he was diagnosed with “mild autism” at the age of 4 years and has notable problems with attention. He attends special classes in public school and has mild intellectual disability disorder. She adds that he has a maternal cousin with similar symptoms. Physical examination shows large ears, head, and testes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Fragile X syndrome is an X-linked dominant cause of inherited intellectual disability due to increased CGG repeats in the FMR1 gene. It presents with clinical features including intellectual disability, an elongated face, hyperextensible joints, and macroorchidism.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient's constellation of findings, including intellectual disability, an elongated face, large ears, and hyperextensible joints may also present with hyperextensible joints and a high-arched palate. Fragile X syndrome is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability after Down syndrome and is inherited in an X-linked dominant pattern. It is caused by a CGG repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene. On karyotype, the end of the X chromosome appears truncated, leading to the name fragile X. Patients are at increased risk for mitral valve prolapse and educational difficulties. They are often diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder. As anticipation is seen, wherein future generations have increased severity and/or earlier onset of disease.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Congenital rubella (Choice A) presents in neonates with the classic triad of cataracts, sensorineural deafness, and cardiac defects, not intellectual disability presenting in childhood.

MECP2 gene mutation (Choice C) is the cause of Rett syndrome, characterized by regression of development after apparently normal development, patients subsequently lose speech capabilities and motor skills, and developmental delay. Autistic features may be present but abnormal facial features are not seen.

Microdeletion on chromosome 7 (Choice D), specifically 7q11.23, is the cause of Williams syndrome. In addition to intellectual delay, elfin facies, short stature, vascular and cardiac valve abnormalities are seen. Patients have intellectual disability, friendly, social, and endocrine abnormalities (eg, hypothyroidism).

Prenatal alcohol exposure (Choice E) leads to irreversible fetal neurologic effects and physical malformations. Due to failure of cell migration, fetal alcohol syndrome presents with physical abnormalities such as smooth philtrum, thin upper border, cleft lip/palate, smooth or long philtrum), limb deformities, and congenital heart disease.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation and incorrect answer wording is OCR-corrupted; preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.retrievalTag: Conversion disorder after minor car collision
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after she was involved in a motor vehicle collision. She was the restrained front-seat passenger in a car that was struck from behind by a truck traveling at 5 mph. She remembers no significant jolting of her body as a result of the collision. Ten minutes after the collision, she developed weakness and loss of sensation on the left side of her body. Examination shows decreased visual acuity in the left eye. Senses of hearing, olfaction, and taste are decreased on the left side. She does not voluntarily move the left side of her face, and she has no voluntary movement of her left extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally in all extremities. Babinski sign is absent. Sensation to vibration is decreased more on the left side of the forehead than on the right. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Conversion disorder, also known as functional neurological symptom disorder, involves neurologic symptoms that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. Conversion is postulated to represent the subconscious conversion of intolerable emotional distress into neurologic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Conversion disorder, also known as functional neurological symptom disorder, involves neurologic symptoms that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. This patient demonstrates midline splitting and sensory loss localized and therefore represents a classic symptom of conversion disorder, along with facial paralysis with Bell palsy rather than a central nervous system lesion. Finally, her left-sided hemiplegia (eg, lack of movement or flickering of muscles) cannot be localized (eg, brainstem lesions typically lead to ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits and contralateral body deficits). Conversion disorder is postulated to represent the subconscious conversion of intolerable emotional distress (eg, vehicle collision) into neurologic symptoms. The pathogenesis likely involves the top-down amplification of emotions. Physicians should conduct a thorough history and physical examination as well as appropriate diagnostic testing to assess for neurologic disease that may explain or partially explain the symptoms when the diagnosis is unclear. Once established, treatment of conversion disorder involves education about the diagnosis, physical therapy, and psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Carotid artery dissection (Choice A), which can occur secondary to whiplash injury, commonly leads to ischemic stroke against the carotid sinus baroreceptors. However, this patient does not demonstrate a localizable neurologic deficit.

Somatization disorder (Choice C), also known as somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. This patient has not been experiencing persistent somatic symptoms are neurologic in nature, conversion disorder is a more precise diagnosis than somatic symptom disorder.

Subdural hematoma (Choice D) is defined by bleeding between the dura and arachnoid mater. In severe cases, brain parenchyma, which can result in progressive neurologic deficits with upper motor neuron signs, brain herniation syndromes, and signs of increased intracranial pressure (eg, papilledema). This patient's symptoms are atypical for intracranial pressure, altered mental status, or other signs of a large subdural hematoma.

Vertebral artery dissection (Choice E), which rarely occurs secondary to whiplash injury, commonly leads to ischemic stroke and typically present with vestibulocerebellar signs (ataxia, dysmetria, dizziness, imbalance, vertigo), ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits and contralateral decreased pain and temperature sensation, contralateral homonymous hemianopsia. This patient lacks vestibulocerebellar signs, and her neurologic symptoms are ipsilateral.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation sentences are partially OCR-corrupted and preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for advice about a weight loss program. She has a history of major depressive disorder. Her last episode was 16 months ago, and she was treated with bupropion until 7 months ago. She currently takes no medications. She has been employed as a bank manager for the past 10 years. She is wearing multiple rings on each hand and has four piercings in each ear; she states that six of the piercings were done in the past 3 weeks. She is 160 cm (5 ft3 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (119 Ib); BMI is 21 kg/m?. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, her eye contact is intense, and her speech is pressured. She says, "Your nurse tells me my blood pressure and weight are perfect, but | want to make some changes in my life, including slimming down. I've become bored at the bank. | know your sister is a designer, and | love design. Can | call her and ask her where | can get top-notch training?" Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Acute hypomania and mania typically manifest with an irritable or euphoric mood, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Patients with unipolar depression may later develop hypomanic or manic episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient with a history of recurrent depressive episodes is presenting with new-onset hypomanic symptoms. Bipolar disorder, type I, is a mood disorder featuring acute episodes of mania and may include episodes of depression; type II consists of episodes of major depressive disorder and hypomania. Patients with bipolar disorder commonly are initially diagnosed with major depressive disorder before experiencing hypomanic/manic episodes. Hypomanic episodes are like manic episodes, with four to five of the following symptoms: irritable or euphoric mood, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and grandiose thinking. Hypomanic episodes are like manic episodes without psychotic changes when speaking, and increased goal-directed activity (wanting to train in design school). Treatment of bipolar disorder involves mood stabilizing therapy (eg, mood stabilizers or mood stabilizing atypical antipsychotics).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Bulimia nervosa (Choice B) involves cycles of uncontrollable eating and compensatory behaviors such as purging. This patient does not demonstrate recurrent disordered eating behaviors. Her focus on losing weight is in response to another's suggestion.

Cyclothymic disorder (Choice C) presents with hypomanic and depressive symptoms that fail to meet full criteria for either. This patient's symptoms meet criteria for a hypomanic episode, and she has a history of major depressive episodes.

Delusional disorder, erotomanic type (Choice D) presents with fixed delusions of a sexual nature in the absence of disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, depression, or mania. This patient demonstrates clear manic symptoms.

Histrionic personality disorder (Choice E), a cluster B personality disorder, is characterized by theatrical, seductive behavior and attention from others to fulfill emotional needs. Narcissistic personality disorder (Choice F) is characterized by grandiosity, need for admiration and compensatory arrogant, self-aggrandizing behavior to gain admiration, sometimes at others' expense, whereas this patient's dramatic grandiosity is acute and occurring in the setting of manic symptoms.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation wording is partially OCR-corrupted and preserved as best-effort visible text.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of severe anxiety, palpitations, and the sensation of a racing heart. Two months ago, she had the onset of anxiety and insomnia after she and her husband separated. She received a prescription for lorazepam at that time. She reports that the medication calmed her and helped her sleep. She says she occasionally took extra pills and finished the prescription before she could obtain a refill. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. She does not drink alcohol or use ilicit drugs. She is diaphoretic. Her pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows bilateral hand tremors. On mental status examination, she has an anxious mood and irritable affect. Dysregulation of which of the following neurotransmitters is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Benzodiazepine withdrawal is associated with sympathetic overdrive and presentation can include anxiety, insomnia, and diaphoresis.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines such as lorazepam increase the central nervous system activity of γ-aminobutyric acid and therefore lead to relative excess of excitatory pathways. Benzodiazepine withdrawal typically causes anxiety and diaphoresis, which can occur hours after the cessation of use. Withdrawal is managed supportively in severe symptoms of withdrawal, intravenous fluids, nutritional supplementation (especially if alcohol use), and monitoring for complications of benzodiazepine withdrawal include hallucinosis (auditory or visual hallucinations) and seizures.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Dysregulation of acetylcholine (Choice A), such as with organophosphates or other inhibitors of acetylcholinesterase, causes neuromuscular junction. This can result in vomiting, diarrhea, urination, diaphoresis, salivation, lacrimation, bronchorrhea, and muscle weakness.
Dopamine (Choice C) dysregulation, such as with cocaine use, causes increased synaptic dopamine, which leads to euphoria.

Chronic opiate use can lead to inhibition of endogenous opioid (Choice D). Opiate withdrawal presents with dysphoria, tremors, muscle cramping, abdominal cramping, nausea and vomiting, and diarrhea. Lacrimation and piloerection.

Serotonin syndrome is the result of serotonin toxicity (Choice E), which can lead to the triad of acute mental status changes, autonomic dysfunction, and neuromuscular excitability (tremor, myoclonus, hyperreflexia). Due to serotonin receptor dysregulation.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation wording is partially OCR-corrupted.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-month history of constipation. During this time, she has had two bowel movements weekly; they are uncomfortably hard and require straining. Prior to 2 months ago, her bowel movements were normal. The patient says she is in the midst of a divorce and has not been sleeping well because of "stress." She has no other history of serious illness, and her only medication is diphenhydramine as a sleep aid. She follows a well-balanced diet that consists of at least four servings of fresh fruit and vegetables daily. The patient does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use illicit drugs. She appears anxious. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 64 kg (141 Ib); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Diphenhydramine is an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity sometimes used as a sleep aid. In the case of severe or bothersome constipation, diphenhydramine should be discontinued.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: This patient is likely experiencing constipation as an adverse effect of diphenhydramine. Thus, discontinuation of diphenhydramine is an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity used in the treatment of allergic reactions and insomnia. Because acetylcholine binding to muscarinic receptors causes smooth muscle contraction of the sweat glands and activates cholinergic neurons projecting to the cerebral cortex mediate attention, learning, and memory. Antagonism of the muscarinic receptors in the brain and causes the dysfunction of smooth muscle contraction, leading to cognitive impairment, sedation, tachycardia, mydriasis, spasm, urinary retention, and/or constipation. Common constipation symptoms include having uncomfortably hard stools, straining to defecate, and experiencing abdominal bloating. When medications cause bothersome constipation, they should be decreased or discontinued.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Anorectal manometry (Choice A) evaluates the function of the anal sphincter. Colon transit studies (Choice B) can be helpful in the evaluation of constipation. However, this patient has a likely cause of constipation, so these tests are unnecessary before discontinuing diphenhydramine.
Initiation of fiber supplementation (Choice D) can help with idiopathic, chronic constipation. However, the patient has a likely medication cause.

X-ray of the abdomen (Choice E) can assess stool burden. It can be helpful in confirming a diagnosis of constipation but is not indicated when constipation can be diagnosed clinically given her classic symptoms of constipation.

extractionWarnings: Some explanation wording is partially OCR-corrupted.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man with schizophrenia comes to the physician for a haloperidol injection he receives every 3 weeks. He is cooperative and friendly but appears anxious and jittery. He says that he has been hearing voices more frequently over the past week, and that this is affecting his job performance. He works at a local sheltered workshop and lives with his parents. Ten days ago, he got into an argument with his parents about his illness. They repeatedly tell him that he needs to get a regular job or he will "never amount to anything.” They say that the voices he hears are an excuse not to meet new people, and they worry that he will never marry. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Recommend that he move to a halfway house

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Add lithium carbonate to his current regimen

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Add lorazepam to his current regimen

questions.educationalObjective: Though antipsychotic medications are the mainstay of schizophrenia treatment, family conflict is a risk factor for psychotic symptom exacerbations, family therapy can be beneficial.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: According to the DSM-5, patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). To meet diagnostic criteria, psychotic symptoms must exceed 6 months. Family conflict and other psychological stressors are risk factors for psychotic exacerbation. Negative interpretations of hallucinations and decrease patients' emotional resources for effectively coping. Treatment of schizophrenia requires antipsychotic medications, though adjunctive psychosocial interventions and social skills training can be beneficial. Family therapy focuses on psychoeducation, encouraging effective communication, and coping skills for the patient and family to cope with psychotic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Recommending partial hospitalization program (Choice A) is not necessary currently. First, the physician should focus on outpatient basis, such as family conflict.
Recommending that he move to a halfway house (Choice B) is not appropriate. This patient has no criminal history and has a family.

Adding lithium carbonate to his current regimen (Choice D) is unlikely to address this patient's psychotic symptoms. Lithium is effective for manic mood symptoms, but lithium carbonate is unhelpful in schizophrenia.

Adding lorazepam to his current regimen (Choice E) may help assuage anxiety symptoms but does not address the cause of the psychosis exacerbation, the family conflict. Family therapy is more likely to be helpful.

Discontinuing haloperidol and beginning clozapine (Choice F) is not necessary currently. This patient's current mild psychotic exacerbation should be addressed first. Following this, the physician should consider increasing the dosage of haloperidol (Choice G) if symptoms persist.

extractionWarnings: Several stem and explanation phrases are OCR-corrupted and preserved as best-effort visible text.
